did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning classifiers (specifically an ensemble of Multilayer Perceptron and Random Forest) combined with feature extraction techniques (Thepade's SBTC and GLCM) to classify melanoma skin cancer images from the HAM-10000 dataset into benign and malignant classes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving feature extraction (Thepade's SBTC and GLCM) and classification using machine learning algorithms (Multilayer Perceptron, Random Forest, etc.) on the HAM-10000 dataset. It reports specific accuracy results (88.46%) and includes sections for Introduction, Related Work, Proposed Methodology, Experimental Setup, Results, and Conclusion, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Enhanced Melanoma Skin Cancer Detection through Ensemble of Thepade's SBTC and GLCM Features Using Machine Learning
journal: 2025 9th International Conference on Computing, Communication, Control and Automation (ICCUBEA)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma skin cancer detection by proposing an ensemble machine learning approach using Thepade's SBTC and GLCM features. The researchers utilized the HAM-10000 dataset, comprising 6705 benign and 1113 malignant images, to extract color and textural features. These features were evaluated using twenty-five different machine learning classifiers implemented in WEKA. The experimental results demonstrated that fusing Thepade's SBTC with GLCM features and classifying them via an ensemble of Multilayer Perceptron and Random Forest yielded the highest performance. Specifically, the proposed ensemble method achieved an accuracy of 88.46%, outperforming individual feature extraction methods and other classifiers. The findings underscore the effectiveness of combining these specific feature extraction techniques with ensemble learning for improved melanoma classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as benign in the HAM-10000 dataset, comprising 6705 images.
Classified as malignant in the HAM-10000 dataset, comprising 1113 images. The paper focuses on detecting melanoma skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Benign vs Malignant melanoma skin cancer classes
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between benign and malignant melanoma images using the HAM-10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Benign or Malignant) using an ensemble of Multilayer Perceptron and Random Forest classifiers on features extracted via Thepade's SBTC and GLCM. The primary task is classification, evidenced by the use of accuracy metrics (88.46%) for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: HAM-10000
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic skin lesion images used for melanoma detection. The study utilized this dataset to extract color and textural features for classification.

use.training: Features were extracted from the dataset and fed into twenty-five different machine learning classifiers for training purposes.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the performance of machine learning classifiers on the HAM-10000 dataset.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy metrics via 10-fold cross-validation.

HAM-10000.experiment: Feature Extraction and Classification using WEKA
HAM-10000.notes: The paper reports that combining TSBTC with GLCM resulted in higher classification accuracy compared to using TSBTC or GLCM separately. The ensemble of Multilayer Perceptron and Random Forest with fused features achieved the highest accuracy of 88.462%. Individual feature extraction results: GLCM achieved 86.33% with Random Forest (Angle 0, Distance 15); TSBTC achieved 87.516% with Multilayer Perceptron (n=7). Table II details TSBTC accuracy across 2-ary to 9-ary configurations for various classifiers. Table III details GLCM accuracy across different distances (1, 3, 5) and angles (0, 90, 135) for various classifiers.

architecture_used.main_model: Ensemble of Multilayer Perceptron (MLP) and Random Forest (RF)

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Fusion of Thepade's Sorted Block Truncation Coding (SBTC) and Grey Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)
architecture_details.sbtc_details: Extracts color attributes from RGB planes; varying degrees from 2-ary to 10-ary (n-ary); optimal n=7 for MLP
architecture_details.glcm_details: Extracts textural features (Energy, Homogeneity, Dissimilarity, Correlation, Contrast); optimal parameters: Angle 0, Distance 15 for Random Forest
architecture_details.ensemble_method: Fusion of features from TSBTC and GLCM fed into an ensemble classifier combining Multilayer Perceptron and Random Forest

training_details.hardware: Windows OS, 8GB RAM, Intel Core i5 processor

preprocessing_techniques: The paper did not report specific image preprocessing techniques (such as resizing, normalization, or artifact removal) applied to the HAM-10000 dataset before feature extraction. The methodology focuses on feature extraction using Thepade's SBTC (isolating RGB color planes and sorting intensity values) and GLCM (calculating textural features like energy, homogeneity, etc.) directly on the dataset images.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper mentions that data augmentation was applied to enhance the robustness of training in the context of related work (e.g., paper [2] using rotation, flipping, scaling, and translating; paper [3] using augmentation; paper [6] using 10,000 augmented images). However, for the specific experiments conducted in this study using the HAM-10000 dataset, the paper did not report applying any specific data augmentation techniques. The methodology focused on feature extraction (TSBTC and GLCM) and classification without explicit augmentation steps for the primary experiment.

conclusions: The proposed machine learning technique, which fuses Thepade's SBTC and GLCM features, achieves higher accuracy in melanoma detection compared to using individual feature extraction methods.
On the HAM-10000 dataset, the GLCM feature extraction method achieved a maximum accuracy of 86.33% when paired with the Random Forest classifier.
The Thepade's SBTC feature extraction method achieved a maximum accuracy of 87.516% (reported as 87.5% in conclusion) when paired with the Multilayer Perceptron classifier.

The ensemble of Multilayer Perceptron and Random Forest classifiers, utilizing fused Thepade's SBTC and GLCM features, achieved the highest accuracy of 88.462% (reported as 88.4% in conclusion), outperforming individual feature-based identification.
The study underscores the effectiveness of combining Thepade's SBTC with GLCM features for improved melanoma classification into benign and malignant classes.
Future work should explore further experimentation with ensemble machine learning algorithms to enhance the performance of the proposed fusion technique.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (Benign vs. Malignant) using only the HAM-10000 dataset, ignoring the other six lesion classes present in the dataset, which limits the generalizability of the model to broader dermatological contexts.

The paper does not report standard deviation or confidence intervals for the accuracy metrics, relying solely on point estimates from 10-fold cross-validation, which prevents assessment of result stability.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the significance of the performance difference between the proposed ensemble method and baseline classifiers.

The methodology relies on hand-crafted feature extraction (Thepade's SBTC and GLCM) rather than deep learning feature learning, which may be less robust to variations in image quality, lighting, and artifact presence compared to modern CNN-based approaches.
The study lacks external validation on independent datasets, raising concerns about potential overfitting to the specific characteristics of the HAM-10000 dataset.

The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, or precision metrics, making it difficult to evaluate the clinical trade-off between false positives and false negatives, which is critical for melanoma detection.

The hardware specifications (8GB RAM, Intel Core i5) are modest, and computational complexity or inference time is not reported, limiting the assessment of deployment feasibility in resource-constrained clinical settings.

The feature extraction process described involves sorting intensity values in columns, which may be sensitive to image orientation and resolution if not standardized, though specific preprocessing steps are not detailed.
